FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Data Release 5.0; 3687 early adolescents from 21 U.S. recruitment sites, baseline ages 9-10.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using SEM in Mplus to prospectively examine associations between sleep/sensation-seeking and mature screen usage.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed original article in Sleep (2025); no duplicate identified.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific variable appears as IV or DV in any analysis. Predictors are objective sleep measures (duration, bedtime, variability, social jetlag) and sensation-seeking. Outcomes are mature-rated video gaming and R-rated movie watching. No time-on-phone, time-on-SM, named platform, texting, video chat, MPIQ, SMAQ, or other phone/SM-specific variable has its own coefficient anywhere in main tables, Table 2, Table 3, or any sensitivity. Step 2 not needed. Per binding clarification 4, mature-rated/R-rated content alone does not qualify as a phone/SM variable. FT-IC2 = NOT_MET.

notes: Paper examines sleep and sensation-seeking predicting mature-rated video games and R-rated movies. Content-rating variables without any phone/SM-specific variable anywhere in analyses. Excluded under FT-EC2.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, EARS subsample (n=495 Android users at Year-4 follow-up); Data Release 5.1 referenced.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis comparing EARS passive smartphone tracking vs. self-reported screen time and testing associations with behavioral problems and cognitive outcomes.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in npj Mental Health Research (2025); no duplicate identified.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: EARS-logged social media time (phone-specific) appears as IV with individual-trait outcomes on the other side: externalizing symptoms (beta=0.07, 95%CI:0.03-0.12), internalizing symptoms (self-reported SM only, beta=0.05, 95%CI:0.01-0.09), SMAQ (beta=0.06, 95%CI:0.01-0.11), and picture vocabulary scores (OR=0.96, 95%CI:0.93-0.99 for under-reporting in Table 3b). Self-reported social media time similarly paired with externalizing (beta=0.06, 95%CI:0.01-0.11) and internalizing (beta=0.05, 95%CI:0.01-0.09) symptoms. Phone-specific SM (EARS) paired with individual-trait outcomes (behavioral problems, cognitive measures) qualifies. MET at Step 1.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple beta coefficients, ORs with 95% CIs reported throughout (Table 3a, 3b, Figure 3) for associations between social media measures and individual-trait outcomes.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis performed; numeric estimates reported for SM-outcome relationships throughout.

FT_EC7_reason: Social media appears as IV (predicting externalizing/internalizing symptoms and cognitive outcomes) in multiple analyses. SM is not DV-only; EC7 does not apply.
notes: Paper compares EARS-logged vs. self-reported SM time and tests associations with behavioral/cognitive outcomes. Both objective and subjective SM measures serve as IV predicting health outcomes. INCLUDE.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study (Data Release 4.0); 10,757 participants from 21 U.S. sites, ages 10-14, baseline 2016-2018.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using mixed-effects models to prospectively examine parental monitoring and family conflict associations with screen time across six modalities.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Acta Paediatrica (2024); no duplicate identified.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Phone/SM-specific variables (texting, video chat, social media) each appear as separate DVs with their own B coefficients in Table 2, paired with parental monitoring and family conflict on the other side. Family conflict predicts social media B=0.008 (95%CI:0.002,0.04) at Year 1; parental monitoring predicts texting B=-0.0005 (95%CI:-0.01,0.01) and video chat at Year 2 (Table 2). Individual modality breakouts include texting (phone-specific), video chat (phone-specific), and social media. The other side (parental monitoring, family conflict) qualifies as individual-trait-level per criteria (parenting practices, family functioning). MET at Step 1.

FT_IC3.evidence: B coefficients with 95% CIs reported in Table 2 for all six screen modalities including texting, video chat, and social media.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential mixed-effects models with numeric estimates reported for SM/texting/video chat outcomes.

FT_EC7_reason: All three conditions met: (a) longitudinal design (baseline predictors -> Year 1 and Year 2 screen time outcomes); (b) screen time including social media, texting, and video chat is always the DV; (c) no analysis anywhere in the paper uses SM/texting/video chat as IV. Baseline screen time appears only as a covariate in Model 2, not as an outcome in a cross-sectional analysis. No cross-sectional analysis with SM as DV is presented. FT-EC7 applies.
notes: Paper examines parental monitoring and family conflict (IVs) prospectively predicting screen time modalities including social media, texting, and video chat (always DVs). No SM-as-IV analysis anywhere. No cross-sectional SM-as-DV analysis. FT-EC7 exclusion applies.
